I'm not sure its entirely avoidable: other files throughout the runtime inevitably call `getTracer().newSpan()` regardless of whether OpenTelemetry is enabled or not. That's the API pattern OTel expects -- everything is a no-op unless initialized, so client code should look the same whether or not tracing is occurring.

We could add a shim over the OTel API to create our own no-import, no-op trace API but we'd probably sacrifice a lot of conveniences of the OTel API. Happy to discuss further if you're unsatisfied with my justification :)

I was looking for a tool like this to automate example snippets within the doc-string.  My idea about docs is a little different than what you proposed above.  I think docs should be part of the code and not outside of it.   When writing docs, I want to include example snippets within class doc-string.  But problem is, those snippets may soon run out of sync with the actual code.  So all snippets must be within a directory, say `snippets` or we can re-use `examples` folder for it.  Then, I want to simply add something like this in the class doc-string

```
.. snippet::

   examples/snippets/acceptors.py#L10-L13
```

and this should render into actual code snippet either embedded via GitHub APIs or embedded during doc generation. 
 We can write tests for snippets too.  This will ensure that doc readers never run into surprises because I missed updating the doc somewhere :)

`_chosen_device` is the method called by the `OTPTokenForm._clean_otp` method in django-otp.

It currently returns None which makes django-otp try to match the token against each user's devices in order until one works (in `match_token`) but since `match_token` uses `devices_for_token` that iterates over the devices that way `for model in device_classes():` there is no guarantee that it won't try the backup devices first.

Arrow column names are always strings, but this doesn't apply to Pandas. e.g.

```python
df = pd.DataFrame({1: np.random.rand(10), 2: np.random.rand(10)})
table = pa.table(df)
```

We can access columns of the above arrow table via `table[0]`, `table[1]`, `table["1"]`, `table["2"]`, but not `table["0"]` or `table[2]`.

There are many places that assume that column names of a Ray dataset are strings. Update or redesign is needed.

Yeah, that is a known issue. The `default_collate` internally uses [`torch.stack`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/9c036aa112b0a8fd9afb824d1fda058e2b66ba1d/torch/utils/data/_utils/collate.py#L143). Since we only automatically wrap on `.to()` and `.clone()` this will drop the feature type.

Three options here:

1. Allow `torch.stack` to retain the feature type. I would not go that route, since we cannot know in general if the result of a stack is still the feature we started out with. We at least need more elaborate checks that make sure this is happening.
2. Fix `default_collate` to handle our feature types. It is a possible solution, but won't happen, because the feature types are `torchvision` only and `default_collate` is a general solution.
3. Provide a custom collate like `vision_collate`. This would mean that depending on the taks, users can no longer go with the default value in the `DataLoader`, but judging from our references, this seems to be somewhat common. Plus, this `vision_collate` could also include more functionality that we need:
    1. Don't `torch.stack` images if we find `BoundingBox`'es.
    2. Support for `None` that some of our datasets already return (#5233) and will be heavily utilized by the new datasets for missing data in a configuration.

My vote is out for 3. To avoid rewriting `default_collate` from scratch in `torchvision` and thus risking it going out of sync with the core version, I made a push to make `default_collate` more extensible in [this gist](https://gist.github.com/pmeier/da684e84283113ed6e427d6519d21efe). @ejguan wanted to handle this from the `torchdata` side. Any progress there?

Absolutely. This is the basic logic we have in .NET config provider:
We keep a list of secret clients - this includes user created clients and the ones we create using user provided credentials.
When we are trying to resolve a secret, we first search in our list of secret clients to see if there is a matching client already added. If yes, use it. If not, create a new one using user's credentials and add it to the list.

If there is no matching client found after the above steps, we check if there is any secret resolver. If yes, use secret resolver. If no, throw exception.

If there is a matching client, use that client to call get_secret.

Here's the code (you can ignore the secret cache logic because we dont have it here right now): https://github.com/Azure/AppConfiguration-DotnetProvider/blob/1f3f65bb6aa94f66ab62085d87f794e376585e0c/src/Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.AzureAppConfiguration/AzureKeyVaultReference/AzureKeyVaultSecretProvider.cs#L48-L70

```py
import hashlib
import pathlib

from torchvision.prototype import datasets


next(iter(datasets.load("lsun", split="train", skip_integrity_check=True)))


def sha256sum(path, chunk_size=1024 * 1024):
    checksum = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(chunk_size), b""):
            checksum.update(chunk)
    return checksum.hexdigest()


for path in pathlib.Path(datasets.home()).joinpath("lsun").glob("*_train_lmdb.zip"):
    print(path.name, sha256sum(path))
```

I'm confused how you managed to end up with a worker with "no pieces of the eval sets ended up on that worker"? It should be the case that if no pieces of the eval set end up on rank 0, this would cause the test to fail because `dask.py` throws the error when it finds out that any worker is allocated 0 partitions of eval_set data.

The rank 0 thing raises the issue that perhaps one sub-dataset of an eval_set whose length is > 1 might not receive any parts of a particular sub-dataset, in which case `len(evals_result)` could be `< len(eval_set)`, for example, if there are two `(X, y)` pairs in `eval_set` but the rank 0 worker only gets parts allocated to it from a single pair. I don't want to nitpick here but I think the design could be improved. I don't see a reason why the datetime object should be passed to the adapter constructor. Rather, I would like to instantiate the adapter instance once and parametrize the operation that should be performed:

```python
import datetime, date, timedelta
from thoth.storages import AdvisersResultsStore

adviser = AdvisersResultsStore()
adviser.connect()

#
# All the documents available.
#
for idd in adviser.get_document_listing(): 
    print(idd)

#
# All the documents created in the past 30 days.
#
datetime_last30 = datetime.datetime.utcnow() - datetime.timedelta(days=30)
for idd in adviser.get_document_listing(start_datetime=datetime_last30):
    print(idd)

#
# All the documents created last month.
#
last_month_start = date.today().replace(day=1) - timedelta(days=1)
last_month_end = date.today().replace(day=1) - timedelta(days=last_month_start.day)
for idd in adviser.get_document_listing(start_datetime=datetime_, end_datetime=last_month_end):
    print(idd)
```

I've taken a look at the `difflib` module, and it's based on another algorithm (substring matching).
I think the one currently implemented works better to correct typos for two reasons:
* The algorithm difflib uses returns a score that depends on the length of the strings matched, whereas we mostly care about the absolute number of differences, and it doesn't consider character swap as a single change.  
* The current implementation should be more efficient on long strings because we can prune results early by giving a max distance of 1 or 2. That makes the matching mostly O(len(text)) whereas difflib is O(len(text) * len(pattern) * nb(pattern))

* The difference between the __before_script__ section and the __script__ section is that when a test returns zero then Travis CI hides the output of that test under a twisty which does not visually clutter Travis report.
* Running flake8 on multiple versions of Python 3 will return similar results because they all share a common Abstract Syntax Tree.  No need to burn up CPUs on redundant tests. Python 3.7 was selected because it is the most restrictive with the addition of __async__ and __await__ as reserved words in the language.
* Putting the flake8 configuration in some separate file has two downsides:
    1. It removes the flexibility to have [multiple flake8 runs](https://github.com/vnpy/vnpy/blob/master/.travis.yml#L14): One for the showstopper issues and the other with __--exit-zero__.
    2. When our contributors want to understand what __flake8__ means, they will need to look for __.flake8__ (they won't find one) then __tox.ini  (they won't find one)and then finally read through __setup.cfg__ to see if there is a __[flake8]__ section.  _Explicit is better than implicit._
* The comment does not say what flake8 is (a linter) but explains what these command line parameters are asking flake8 to do for us.  flake8 can do tons of style validation (see the second pass in the vnpy example above) but we are not asking it to do any of that in this invocation.
* I think the current PR title more accurately describes what flake8 is doing in this PR.  We are NOT merely doing __flake8 .__ with no command line options.

I agree that predicates should be limited to just the spending account for a transaction, if only because it's guaranteed to be loaded into RAM already as part of checking its tx fee balance (and thus very cheap to examine).  Agreed that the predicate should not involve other accounts in order to keep the complexity down.

I'm not sure if we should rule out predicates on the _state_ of the spending account, however.  I think we should consider supporting checks on both _spending events_ and _account state_.  For example, if I'm executing a smart contract that allows me to buy a BNS name for some STX, then the predicate should be something like "the spending account now owns this name" instead of "a name was sent to the spending account".  The reason is that the _event_ "a name was sent to the spending account" does _not_ reflect the post-condition the user cares about, since it is possible that the user calls a malicious smart contract method to buy the name -- the transaction code the user executes could transfer the name from the seller to their account, and then transfers both the STX and the name from their account back to the seller.  A predicate about their _account state_ would prevent such a transaction from executing, but a predicate about their _spending event_ would allow it.

```suggestion
What's the difference between a worker and an actor?

Each "Ray worker" is a python process. 

Workers are treated differently for tasks and actors. Any "Ray worker" is either 1. used to execute multiple Ray tasks or 2. is started as a dedicated Ray actor.

 * Tasks: When Ray starts on a machine, a number of Ray workers will be started automatically (1 per CPU by default). They will be used to execute tasks (like a process pool). If you execute 8 tasks with `num_cpus=2`, and total number of CPUs is 16 (`ray.cluster_resources()["CPU"] == 16`), you will end up with 8 of your 16 workers idling.

 * Actor: A Ray Actor is also a "Ray worker" but is instantiated at runtime (upon `actor_cls.remote()`). All of its methods will run on the same process, using the same resources (designated when defining the Actor). Note that unlike tasks, the python processes that runs Ray Actors are not reused and will be terminated when the Actor is deleted.

To maximally utilize your resources, you want to maximize the time that
your workers are working. You also want to allocate enough cluster resources 
so that both all of your needed actors can run and any other tasks you
define can run. This also implies that tasks are scheduled more flexibly,
and that if you don't need the stateful part of an actor, you're mostly
better off using tasks.
```

Yes, `epoch` is the epoch that just ended.  The idea is that at the end of the epoch, we want to `commit` to save our progress.  We could avoid having to do the `+ 1` here if we called this `on_epoch_begin`, but if we commit before incrementing the epoch number (but after resetting the batch number), then we would end up repeating the last epoch.  So this way we ensure that when we commit, the state is fully up to date in case an error occurs.

The `initial_epoch` is because the `epoch` provided by the callback is a relative epoch (we cannot offset it at an initial epoch).  So if we complete some number of epochs and then reset, we do not want to lose our progress.

I'll also add a comment to the code describing this since it's a bit subtle but here is the gist:

Arrow has both **array-level slicing** and **buffer-level slicing**; both are zero-copy, but the former has a serialization bug where the entire buffer is serialized instead of just the slice, while the latter's serialization works as expected and only serializes the slice of the buffer. I.e., array-level slicing doesn't propagate the slice down to the buffer when serializing the array.

All that these copy methods do is, at serialization time, take the array-level slicing and translate them to buffer-level slicing, so only the buffer slice is sent over the wire instead of the entire buffer.

I actually think it would be quite hard to trigger some sort of refcounting-type issue here. IIUC, it should be pretty much impossible. 

By definition, the object we're accessing is a strongly held attribute of another strongly held object (i.e., in CPython it's in the object's `__dict__`; it PyPy it's possibly not in a literal `dict`, but part of the hidden class backing the object implementation). According to [this PyPy blog post](https://morepypy.blogspot.com/2018/09/inside-cpyext-why-emulating-cpython-c.html), as long as a particular object exists in the Python/PyPy world, the `PyObject*` that points to it will never go invalid, *even if the refcount is 0*:

> We now need a way to convert between W_Root and PyObject* and vice-versa; also, we need to to ensure that the lifetime of the two entities are in sync. In particular:
> 1. as long as the W_Root is kept alive by the GC, we want the PyObject* to live ((even if its refcount drops to 0**;
> 2. as long as the PyObject* has a refcount greater than 0, we want to make sure that the GC does not collect the W_Root.

So here's some fun NgRx behavior that I've been exploring recently: NgRx will deeply call Object.freeze() on all state after your reducer is called.

What it means for you is that, at runtime, this L1104 will silently fail to modify `nextCardStateMap[cardId].timeSelection = linkedTimeSelection;`.

You won't be able to catch this in a test because the freeze is not applied in the tests.

I recommend changing L1101-1104 to:
```
nextCardStateMap[cardId] = {
  ...nextCardStateMap[cardId],
  timeSelection: linkedTimeSelection,
};
```

It has the benefit of actually modifying timeSelection AND it handles the case where nextCardStateMap[cardId] is undefined.

I understand that one could argue that Velocity templates could be considered language-independent. However, given [Apache Velocity](https://velocity.apache.org/) stating "Velocity is a Java-based template engine", it might still be useful to aim for parity with the Java-based reference implementation.

There, only `true` and `false` are accepted boolean values (see pointer in [docs](https://velocity.apache.org/engine/devel/user-guide.html#velocimacro-arguments)) and using `True` or `False` raises an exception (tested with https://github.com/inbravo/apache-velocity-examples). This behavior is independent of the output type (tested using text output, not json).

Hence, given the following template and variables:

```python
template = """
{
#set( $myTrue = true )
"booleanKeyTrue": $booleanKeyTrue,
"booleanKeyFalse": $booleanKeyFalse,
"booleanLiteralTrue": true,
"booleanLiteralFalse": false,
#if( $booleanKeyTrue )
"conditionalOutcome": "PASSED"
#else
"conditionalOutcome": "FAILED"
#end
}
"""
variables = {
    "booleanKeyTrue": "true",
    "booleanKeyFalse": "false",
}
```

The suggested workaround with dumping JSON (`json.dumps(ast.literal_eval(response._content))`) produces the correct output for the content type JSON:

```python
import json

from localstack.utils.aws.templating import VtlTemplate
rendered_template = VtlTemplate().render_vtl(template, variables)
print(rendered_template)
print(json.loads(rendered_template))
```

```json
{
"booleanKeyTrue": true,
"booleanKeyFalse": false,
"booleanLiteralTrue": true,
"booleanLiteralFalse": false,
"conditionalOutcome": "PASSED"
}
```

Nevertheless one could still argue that the underlying airspeed templating output violates the (Java-based) Velocity protocol:

```python
import airspeed
import json

t = airspeed.Template(template)
booleanKeyTrue = True
booleanKeyFalse = False
print(t.merge(locals()))
```

```json
{
"booleanKeyTrue": True,
"booleanKeyFalse": False,
"booleanLiteralTrue": true,
"booleanLiteralFalse": false,
"conditionalOutcome": "PASSED"
}
```

```suggestion
Retrying tasks
##############

In order to retry a failed task, the task must be re-executed with a matching ``task_id``,
as shown below:

.. code-block:: python

    @app.task(base=Batches, flush_every=100, flush_interval=10)
    def my_task(requests):
        for request in requests:
            # Do things which might fail.
            result = ...
            if not result:
                # Retry the task with the same arguments and task_id, 10 seconds from now.
                my_task.apply_async(
                    args=request.args,
                    kwargs=request.kwargs,
                    countdown=10,
                    task_id=request.id,
                )
```

View change completion is the moment when replicas get to know their primaries. At this moment the node should check the current statuses of connections to the primaries (since the node may already be disconnected from another node which becomes the primary in some instance in the new view). For the master replica this check is redundant because if its primary were disconnected then the node would not receive `ViewChangeDone` message and thus would not complete the view change. But for backup replicas this check on view change completion is required. It is also used when the node starts (completes primaries selection for the view 0).

That way the logging configuration module doesn't need to strictly depend on (import) the libraries it is doing logging for. For example, maybe beamlines sometimes use pyepics and sometimes use caproto. If I set up logging like:

```py
from pyepics import ...
from caproto import ...
```

I need to have both installed. If I set up logging like

```py
import logging
logging.getLogger('pyepics')
logging.getLogger('caproto')
```
then this will work even if pyepics or caproto are not installed.

Also, by setting up logging before the library is even imported, it ensures that we cannot accidentally miss any important log messages by setting up logging too late.

There are a few ways to handle this:
1. You can get the latest version of the file by downloading [run_python_system_tests.py](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ni/nimi-python/master/tools/run_python_system_tests.py). There are command line utilities wget and curl, or you can write a very simple script in the language of your choice. Curl is part of Windows. At least Windows 10. This is very simple to do. `curl -o test.py https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ni/nimi-python/master/tools/run_python_system_tests.py`
1. You can keep the logic to download and unzip the latest release artifact in your test suite, and then invoke a script in the tools directory to actually run the system tests. This is similar to what we do for running system tests in the CI. `tools/system_tests.bat` is used by the Jenkins jobs to actually invoke the test. This way these jobs don't need to be updated every time the system tests change.
1. A combination of the first two. Move the logic that downloads and unzips the release artifact into one script. This won't change unless GitHub's rest API changes. Have a second script in tools to invoke the system tests that you run from the unzipped artifact. 

I think the last one is the best from a maintenance standpoint. Also, the script you invoke to run the system tests will match the system tests you are running. I.e. the ones in the release artifact.

Commented this below, but to me the cleanest solution would be that training callbacks accept an optional list of result preprocessors in `__init__`. If this is provided, it overrides the default preprocessors that are set. Internally we can just convert this into a SequentialResultsPreprocessor. To avoid making IndexedArrays of IndexedArrays, we should write the simplest code and then call `outputIndexedArray.simplify()`, which collapses IndexedArrays of IndexedArrays. That might also avoid branching—we might be able to do the same thing whether the content is an IndexedArray or not.

The two simplification routines, `IndexedArray::simplify` and `UnionArray::simplify`, are complex with lots of `dynamic_casts` because in general, you need to do that. (`UnionArray::simplify`'s casting is in some of the `merge` implementations.) The reason I wrote them is to concentrate all that special-casing so that we don't have to do it again.

Even if creating an extra IndexedArray or UnionArray and then calling `simplify` to get rid of it is a little slower (less than a factor of 2 or so), it's preferable for keeping the code manageable. We'll be adding a lot of operations, potentially forever, so we have to make sure that adding new ones doesn't scale up the complexity of the project. (I.e. they should become routine.)

The JWT token itself is used only between client and authorisation server, and there is not much sensitive data inside (client id mostly). The point of using it is stronger auth, and not passing the client secret on the wire. It's done via HTTPS anyway, so there is an encryption layer. It must mostly not be leaked to avoid replay attack, but there is protection against that (JTI).
The authorisation server then give an access token (again via HTTPS) which is a random series of bytes, this is the one which will then be used between the client and the resource owner. This one can be revoked.

In short: as long as HTTPS is OK, the token is OK. If HTTPS is broken, we have bigger problems anyway.

This check is still racy.  For example, we can have the sequence:

1. Thread 2 is here and finds `_Py_IsFinalizing() != 0`
2. Main thread starts the late stage of finalization and causes `_Py_IsFinalizing()` to become non-zero.
3. Thread 2 uses `gil_scoped_acquire_simple` and crashes.

I don't believe there is any safe way to use Py_IsFinalizing as you are trying to use it here --- the problem is that you need a way to atomically check that `Py_IsFinalizing` is false *and* acquire the GIL, and furthermore ensure that `_Py_IsFinalizing` remains false until the end of your block.  The Python C API doesn't provide any way to do that directly at the moment --- I addressed that in https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/28525 but unfortunately I haven't heard back from the Python maintainers regarding that PR in a long time.

It is possible to implement a workaround, as is done in TensorStore:

https://github.com/google/tensorstore/blob/fe7aae44a788dbdfc731ea1c00b83f0d51e4f2f4/python/tensorstore/gil_safe.h#L80

It is based on registering an atexit handler so that we can delay finalization while in such a block.

my main concern here is just situations in which we have a `<huge number of partitions>` -> `<unpartitioned asset>` connection, which would require us to fire off a massive query in order to get the latest data versions per each one of those partitions.

it's definitely possible that this is kinda fine in practice, but for this PR I think we can still claim an objective improvement over status quo if we filter out cases where there's an AllPartitionMapping. From the PR descrption
> This is true except for the last commit (6374839) where there is a backward incompatible change (from commit message)
> 
> > Remove check for additional properties while creating model
> > This is needed to reduce even more the difference in Model vs dict creation.
> > NOTE: Once a model is created there are no checks in case additional properties would have been added.

Essentially before this PR was not possible to create a model defining additional properties if the model spec does specify that no additional properties are accepted.
model spec
```yaml
x-model: Model
type: object
properties:
  prop:
    type: integer
additionalProperties: false
```
On master the following python code would raise an exception
```python
m = Model(prop=1, additional=1)
```
but the following code, attempting to do the same change would work
```python
m = Model(prop=1)
m.additional=1
```
I do think that this is an "inconsistent" behaviour and removing this "safety" allows for an marginal performance improvement.

Because on Windows, umask is in practice useless. When you call `os.mkdir`, there is no difference between `0o400` and `0o600`. Folder will be readable and writable for the user who created it. - So then the created target task can [handle the expired lock](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/325f4327583dc8e75677751865e63dda600bd929/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/target.py#L267) and clear it.

This can be done on release build. However, one of the times where code size becomes important is during product development (integration of OT into projects/products) where user may want to have a development/debug build where  asserts are enabled (and maybe even detailed logging) and still want to fit such a build on a resource-limited device (e.g., to use for in-field testing). 

Asserts are useful but it is important to be selective about where/how we use them. For example, if we add an assert on every public OT API to check if the parameter `aInstance` is not null, it increases code size with little practical benefit.

I think we should remove both of these methods from the `Dataset` object, since linked properties are specifically scoped to models. Additionally, linked property names do not have to be unique across datasets, so calling `get_linked_property` with a name will arbitrarily return a single linked property when multiple may exist. For example, the following is valid:
```
a = dataset.create_model('a')
b = dataset.create_model('b')
c = dataset.create_model('c')

lpa = a.add_linked_property('link_to_c', c)
lpb = b.add_linked_property('link_to_c', c)

dataset.get_linked_property('link_to_c') # Could return lpa OR lpb

```

Using job name in this format of `parsl.auto.{}` is awkwardly here: inside linux, the full hostname is set to `parsl.auto.WHATEVER`, which is generally interpreted as the base hostname being `parsl` and the domain name being `auto.WHATEVER`.

This means that tooling which reports a short hostname always reports `parsl` and it is then hard to tell multiple VMs in the same run apart.

Contrast this with AWS which uses a hostname based on the instance's internal IP address, so the short hostname is reported differently for each VM, something like this: `ip-10-0-4-242`

No, that's a bit incorrect. `wheel` is installed automatically inside the build environment, so that one is unnecessary.

`build` is necessary if you're calling it explicitly. You don't list it in `pyproject.toml` because `pyproject.toml` decides what's used by `build`. However, you need to list it in `tox.ini` if you intend to call `pyproject-build` from `tox`.

So basically, tox installs `build` and calls `pyproject-build`. Then `build` installs `setuptools` based on `pyproject.toml`, and calls its backend, and setuptools' backend installs `wheel` automatically.

This generator pattern will crawl through the `_next` links until there are no more mosaics to fetch:
```python
def _get_next_url(resp):
    return resp.json().get('_links', {}).get('_next')

def list_mosaics(self, name__is=None, name__contains=None):
    # yield one mosaic at a time from the first page of results
    url = self.base_url + 'basemaps/v1/mosaics/'
    resp = self._get(url, name__is=name__is, name__contains=name__contains)
    mosaics = resp.json().get('mosaics', [])
    for mosaic in mosaics:
        yield mosaic
    # crawl through next link results, yielding mosaics until there are none left
    next_url = _get_next_url(resp)
    while next_url:
        # no extra params needed, next_url has querystring already
        resp = self._get(next_url)
        mosaics = resp.json().get('mosaics', [])
        for mosaic in mosaics:
            yield mosaic
        next_url = _get_next_url(resp)
```

This operator has `destination_dataset_table` so I tested the following four pattern. I suppose it works as expected.

1. (OK) source dataset in US, destination_dataset in US, no location specified
2. (OK) source dataset in Tokyo, destination_dataset in Tokyo, location specified as `asia-northeast1`
3. (Fail) source dataset in Tokyo, destination_dataset in US, location specified as 'asia-northeast1`
4. (Fail) source dataset in US, destination_dataset in Tokyo, no location specified


```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from airflow import DAG
from operators.bigquery_operator import BigQueryOperator
from airflow.utils.dates import days_ago

ARGS = {
    'owner': 'airflow',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'retries': 0,
    'start_date': days_ago(1)
}

COMMON_PARAMS = {}

with DAG(
    dag_id='test_bq',
    default_args=ARGS,
    params=COMMON_PARAMS,
    schedule_interval='@once') as dag:

    # both datasets are in US
    # this should be OK
    t1 = BigQueryOperator(
        task_id='us_to_us',
        sql='SELECT * FROM test_us_ds.test_table',
        bigquery_conn_id='google_cloud_default',
        destination_dataset_table='fr-stg-datalake:test_us_ds.dest_table_us_to_us'
    )

    # both source dataset and dest dataset are in Tokyo
    # this should be OK
    t2 = BigQueryOperator(
        task_id='tky_to_tky',
        sql='SELECT * FROM test_tokyo_ds.test_table',
        bigquery_conn_id='google_cloud_default',
        location='asia-northeast1',
        destination_dataset_table='fr-stg-datalake:test_tokyo_ds.dest_table_tky_to_tky'
    )

    # source dataset is in Tokyo but dest table is in US
    # this should fail
    t3 = BigQueryOperator(
        task_id='tky_to_us',
        sql='SELECT * FROM test_tokyo_ds.test_table',
        bigquery_conn_id='google_cloud_default',
        location='asia-northeast1',
        destination_dataset_table='fr-stg-datalake:test_us_ds.dest_table_tky_to_us'
    )

    # source dataset is in US but dest table is in Tokyo
    # this should fail
    t4 = BigQueryOperator(
        task_id='us_to_tky',
        sql='SELECT * FROM test_us_ds.test_table',
        bigquery_conn_id='google_cloud_default',
        destination_dataset_table='fr-stg-datalake:test_tokyo_ds.dest_table_us_to_tky'
    )

    t1 >> t2
    t1 >> t3
    t1 >> t4
    globals()[dag] = dag
```

Yes, it would cause every task to re-split the data.  In my experiments re-splitting the data in each task, wasn't that computationally heavy and didn't slow down the tasks. But since you have raised a valid concern, I shall profile the split method.

> I also wonder if this will produce the same splits every time, as it can shuffle the data.

For splitters which are **not ShuffleSplitters**, it will always have the same set of indices, across tasks. 
For splitters **which are ShuffleSplitters**, [we can set an integer `random_state`](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/55bf5d93e5674f13a1134d93a11fd0cd11aabcd1/sklearn/model_selection/_split.py#L407), to maintain the splits across tasks. [[`GroupShuffleSplit`, `ShuffleSplit`, `StratifiedShuffleSplit`]](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/classes.html#module-sklearn.model_selection)

> Or simply have `fun` run all (train, test) splits for an individual grid element. That batches work together more, which is simple and probably faster, but bad if you have a lot of parallelism but few param grid elements, and many folds.

So the challenge here is that `_percent_decode` with `subencoding=False` returns `bytes` instead of `Text` (aka `unicode`).  That would require that we annotate it as `Union[Text, bytes]` and a large number of callers would then have to `cast` the return to `Text` (or `bytes` in the future).

Even in cases where you could previously specify `subencoding=False`, there are cases in this function where you get `Text` instead of `bytes`, which seems problematic.

So what I'd suggest is that when we want to get raw bytes out, that we add a new function that always returns bytes, possibly factoring out the middle bit (for for loop) form here.

There is actually an explanation in the docstring, but I honestly do not know how to describe it concisely. Maybe you can give me suggestions.

Basically, in accordance to a set of rules, there are UPC-A barcodes that may be zero-suppressed (kind of like how some zeroes may be omitted in IPv6), and those have a UPC-E equivalent. The problem is that if you generate certain UPC-E barcodes arbitrarily out of thin air and convert those to UPC-A and back again to UPC-E, there is a possibility the new UPC-E barcode will not be the same as the original.

You can try that quirk [here](https://www.morovia.com/education/utility/upc-ean.asp). The converter accepts a 6 digit UPC-E base, so try converting `100003` to UPC-A. Copy paste the result, and convert it back to UPC-E, and you will get `100000`.

Safe mode basically guarantees that every UPC-E barcode generated, even if such a base was provided explicitly, can be converted to UPC-A and back to UPC-E ad infinitum.

I find this much harder to reason about, I think it's a more risky than the other solutions.

The reason this somewhat works is that it changes the call order in the before case to:
-> t.cancel()
-> Timeout._on_timeout()
-> Timeout._on_exit()
-> Timeout._cancel()  # This is a noop, as it's already exited

I'm not really sure that provides any guarantees, as it's just delaying the cancellation, which happens to be after the exit has already happened.

In the after case, this is not fixed at all (just like all the other attempts), in this case the execution looks like:
-> Timeout._on_timeout()
-> t.cancel()
-> Timeout._cancel()  # Again, we've triggered 2 cancellations in a row
-> Timeout._do_exit()  # No way to know there were 2 cancellations.

There might be a better way of structuring this. In general, encoding multiple bits of information in a dict key is not ideal, since it then has to be re-parsed later to get information out (eg the perturbed site index here).

An alternative would be a more nested dictionary using the site index as a key too.

Since such a dictionary can be verbose to construct, and since `pydash` is in the `atomate` requirements, an alternative syntax can be:

```
from pydash import set_
response_dict = {}
```

and then, later, when you want to use this you can use:

`_set(response_dict, [key, 'Vdn_site', i], val_to_set)`  # returns {key: 'Vdn_site': i: {val_to_set}

No, the point is to not download a page unless we actually need it. Imagine two approaches. First yours:

1. We download each page in self.pages_to_process
2. We hand that off to CL.
3. CL starts downloading the related PDFs
4. After the fourth PDF, CL realizes there's nothing new and stops

Total downloads: self.pages_to_process + four PDFs.

If we use a DeferringList:

1. We download page 1.
2. We hand that to CL
3. CL starts downloading the related PDFs
4. After the fourth PDF, CL realizes there's nothing new, and stops

Total downloads: 1 + four PDFs.

Big picture, the idea is to avoid downloading pages we don't need.

We hard-code the list of dependencies in these scripts instead of doing something like `remotes::install_deps()` because  the last time I checked, that function (and `devtools::install_deps()` before it) passes the option `dependencies` all the way through. So, for example, `remotes::install_deps(dependencies = c("Imports", "Depends", "LinkingTo", "Suggests"))` would install not only all of the `Suggests` of `{lightgbm}`, but also all of the `Suggests` of its dependencies, and so on and so forth. That gets to a very large number of packages pretty quickly, increasing our build times and the risk of failure (especially on Linux, where we have to build packages from source).

That said, I haven't seen any ACME *client* supporting phone numbers (searched for `phone` in some of the most popular ACME client Github repositories). So my toughts here can go two ways:

* There's at least *one* ACME *server* supporting phone contacts, so that may warrant including it in this PR;
* There's no ACME *client* supporting phone contacts, so even if ACME APIs are accepting it, it couldn't have been used anyway, so adding it here would never result in anything. However, I have just checked *a few* clients, maybe there are ACME clients out there supporting phone contacts, I dunno..

Ah yes. Some history about this. It is related to the capacity to import `pywintypes` and `pythoncom`, which are precompiled DLLs that contains symbol and mapping for the data exchanged through the Windows kernel API.

The way the modules are loaded in an application built with pynsist is quite specific because the site-package used to install the application is not very standard. You can see the loader of `sys.path` here: https://github.com/takluyver/pynsist/blob/89431e7c382da103f08c2fe006e388db4d2cf09b/nsist/commands.py#L8-L26

Basically it searches every module in the `pkgs` folder in `INSTALLDIR`, which acts as a "site-package" folder.

Initially with this approach the Python interpreter was not capable to find the DLLs for `pywintypes` and `pythoncom`. The initial script to solve that was taken from the `pynsist` project itself (https://github.com/takluyver/pynsist/blob/master/examples/pywebview/pywin32_paths.py). It was added as a preample script added by pynsist just after the standard `sys.path` loader.

It seems that now this resolution has been integrated in the `pywin32` project. I did not dig enough to fully understand the modifications, but I find a similar machinery for these DLL load under various files installed in the `pkg` folder by `pywin32` (in particular `win32\lib\pywintypes.py` and `pythoncom.py`). Now the old preamble is breaking: it seems that the "let pywin32 does its magic" is now the best approach, and makes a fully functional Certbot as the integration tests show that.

`tokio::process::Child` no longer implements `Future` in Tokio 1.0. Instead, `Child` exposes a `wait` instance method. The problem is that `wait` takes `&mut self` and so the original version of `exit_stream` thus mutably borrows `self` and so cannot be returned from `run_in_workdir` since the borrow checker rejects the code since `Child` does not survive the call to `run_in_workdir`.

The solution is to have `Child` survive that call. This code uses the `ouroboros` crate which has a proc macro that allows for a limited form of self-referential structs. The `Child` struct is owned by this struct and this struct is what comprises part of the stream returned from `run_in_workdir`. The `exit_stream` part of the return value from `run_in_workdir` is also embedded in this struct using the `ouroboros` crate.

Finally, the solution implements `Stream` on `OwnedChild` so that it exposes the same stream as `exit_stream` but as a first-class owner of the embedded `Child`.

After getting some use with the API, I decided to extract the class methods on `LocalWorkspace` into standalone functions `create_stack`, `select_stack` and `create_or_select_stack`. The slightly differing names for the methods on LocalWorkspace felt clunky in the IDE with autocomplete.

I noticed that the [Go API](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/master/sdk/go/x/auto/local_workspace.go#L712-L902) uses factory functions itself, so the discrepancy between language implementations to align with language culture is already present.

We also have the option of overwriting the class method with an instance-specific version as suggested in [this post](https://stackoverflow.com/a/28766809) but I wasn't really loving that.

I think the standalone functions are pythonic, easy to use, are aligned with the method names in `LocalWorkspace` and `Stack` and `auto.create_stack()` reads better than `auto.LocalWorkspace.create[_new]_stack()`.

Happy New Year Bryant @bmoscon 

I have worked hard to improve your new `AsyncConnection` design and I will propose you a nice PR with a new file `runner.py` implementing the class `Runner`. The `Runner` owns the main `message_handler` infinite loop ( and `_watch()`) that was previously the responsibility of `FeedHandler`.

In the coming PR, I have replaced the `Feed.connections()` function by reusing the `address: dict` in the `Feed.__init__()` (first argument). No need to pass `partial(subscribe,x)`
 and `message_handler()` callbacks. The `address: dict` (options and URLs) are used by the `FeedHandler` to create the `AsyncConnection`: each `AsyncConnection` keeps its option/address within its context. This connection context is then accessed by the `Feed` subclass to subscribe to the right pairs/channels.

The `FeedHandler` manages the following objects:
* `Feed` (exchange or provider)
* `AsyncConnection` (Websocket or HTTP)
* `Runner` (one `Runner` per `AsyncConnection`)

The `FeedHandler` has the responsibility to create the `Feed` subsclass, to create its the corresponding `AsyncConnection` according to the `Feed.address` (`str` or `dict`, can be a mix of WS and HTTP), and to create one `Runner` per `AsyncConnection`. Each `Feed` has one or more `AsyncConnection`. Each `Runner` runs only one `AsyncConnection`.

`Runner` knows/calls `Feed` and `AsyncConnection`. But the `Feed` and its subclasses do not know the `Runner`. The `Runner` pass the `AsyncConnection` when calling `Feed.subscribe() and `Feed.handle()`. The `Feed` subclasses use the `AsyncConnection` to access the connection context and to send messages (websocket only). Notice I have renamed `message_handler()` -> `handle()` for consistency with `subscribe() != message_subscriber()`.

Once everything is created, the `FeedHandler` runs all the `Runner` asynchronously. The `Runner` infinite loop calls the `Feed.subscribe(conn)`, and loops over the `AsyncConnection.read()`. Each received message is passed to `Feed.handle(msg,timestamp,conn)`. The `Feed` subcalss has access to `conn.ctx`, the connection context: no need to mange a `seqno_map[conn.id]`. The `conn.ctx` contains the `Feed.address` (and options) used to create the `AsyncConnection`.

All exchanges using websocket benefits this feature:
```py
# slow down the subscription batch by waiting for a response or for a half of a second before next send
try:
    msg = await asyncio.wait_for(self.socket.recv(), timeout=0.5)
    await self.handle(msg, time(), self)
except asyncio.exceptions.TimeoutError:
    pass
```

On shutdown signal, the `FeedHandler` closes the `AsyncConnection`, stops the `Runner` & `Feed`, and waits for termination of all backends before exiting. The `FeedHandler` runs this functions using AsyncIO to speed up the shutdown process. This is important because multiple backends may need to flush their data and the orchestrator (K8s/Docker) may kill Cryptofeed is it takes too much time to terminate. However, even if the backend flushing is faster, `loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(*flush_tasks))` takes some seconds to detect all flushing tasks are completed.

I have adapted almost all exchanges and providers: I have to finish WHALES_ALERT. This improvement of your new design simplifies the source code, especially BITFINEX, BINANCE and more... I have also tested long run with all exchanges/providers activated (except WHALES_ALERT), subscribing to many many pairs per `Feed`, and with backends requiring final flush.

PR will be ready at the end of the week...

There's a small issue here with running `nixio-explore` without a subcommand.  Since it's not marked as required, the `func` attribute isn't set if no subcommand is specified and the `args.func(args)` in main throws an exception.

Adding `required=True` is one solution, but that was added in Python 3.7, so if we want to support 3.6 it's not an option.  The other solution is to add `dest="func"` to `add_subparsers()`, which will create the `func` attribute every time and then in main we can do something like
```python
    parser = create_parser()
    args = parser.parse_args()
    if not args.func:
        parser.print_help()
        sys.exit(1)
    args.func(args)
```

Not quite - because the problem is a little subtler than just that we're upsampling small partitions. The problem has to do with how we're determining the probability when we sample our partitions. We borrow from an algorithm called TeraSort for sampling and splitting partitioned data into bins with a roughly equal amount of data per bin. The algorithm has us sample with probability `(num_partitions / total_data_length) * log(num_partitions * total_data_length)`. This fraction is greater than 1 until `total_data_length ≈ 3 * num_partitions`, so the error we get is because we're trying to sample with probability greater than 1. In the case where `total_data_length < 3 * num_partitions` we are overpartitioned. Concretely, this would be like having 16 rows and having all 16 partitions. In this case, rather than upsampling and using our algorithm, it makes more semantic sense (and is likely faster - although I haven't tested this) to just make 1 axis partition, and sort all of the data, and then repartition (to hopefully get fewer, more filled, partitions). 

In the example you give above, we don't run into the bug, since we'll try and sample each partition with probability 0.04 - so when we get to the partition with skew, there's a 4% chance it will be in our overall sample, which is fine, since it's just 1 data point, and the algorithm will continue with no errors. 

One thing I just noticed though is that this approach only succeeds if we have 1 column partition - it may be better here to borrow from the previous sort by doing an axis partition on the set of row partitions that have the sort column key(s) and then sorting and reindexing, as this code will break if we are block partitioned with more than one column (although that would imply we have a relatively short and wide df, which I imagine are fairly rare).

Replacing this with `magnetSphere.transform.position` works as `transform.position` is already in world space by default, but this doesn't account for if the magnetSphere's capsule collider ever changes its center. To future proof this, I think we should change this to 
```
meta.handSphereCenter = magnetSphere.transform.TransformPoint(magnetSphere.center);
```

This should correctly transform the bounding box center value into word space relative to the game object that it is a component of.

Two thoughts here.

1) I couldn't convince myself quickly that `self.job.message` would have a value here.  Would it be worth checking for that first?

2) I have a general concern with the use of the `job` and `phase` properties here.  My real problem is with how those "properties" are defined, but I don't suggest any changes there for this PR.   Those "properties" are both written to do an `_update()` **every** time they are accessed, and `_update()` hits network at the TAP service job URL.  So for these two lines, we would do 3 GETs of the same information just to craft an error message.  That's not efficient and if the error is related to communication problems with the service, then this makes it worse.

One of the reasons I don't like these properties is that there's no good way to be sure it's safe to use the underscore version (i.e., `_job`, `_phase` which don't do `_updates()`).  In this case I think it would be safe to use the underscore versions on all 3 accesses, but it certain to be safe to leave the `self.phase` in the `if` and use `self._job...` and `self._phase` in the error call since the phase check would have called `_update()`.

I'm thinking in `test.py` we could do one of these two:

1. Choose an arbitrary element of the partitions and determine the environment based on that. Trust that the others are good to go.
2. Determine the environment for each field set in the partition, and raise an AssertionError if they are not all identical because it represents a programmer error in their partition rule

The second is certainly a more developer-friendly approach and will avoid common bugs.

Context:

- In `go` toolchain world, you can set `CGO_ENABLED=0` and prevent any cgo processing from occurring in any transitive dependency. (Bazel rules_go calls this "pure" mode, as in pure Go.) Packages can use the presence or absence of the `cgo` build tag to handle this gracefully.

- For Pants, I would like to add a `cgo_allowed` field to `go_binary` so a user can set `cgo_allowed=False` on a `go_binary` and Pants will disable cgo for all transitive dependencies of that binary. That is https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/16843 which fails with rule graph errors currently.

This PR extracts the option name change from https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/16843.

To me, the word "enable" connotes that something _is_ switched on, whereas "allowed" connotes that possibility that something _may_ be switched on. The latter is what this option is intended to mean.

[PEP8](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#imports) recommends: 

> Absolute imports are recommended, as they are usually more readable and tend to be better behaved (or at least give better error messages) if the import system is incorrectly configured (such as when a directory inside a package ends up on sys.path):

```
import mypkg.sibling
from mypkg import sibling
from mypkg.sibling import example
```

imo it improves readability (tells reader where the module comes from without having to go back to the dir structure) and makes the code easier to maintain when we move files around. Things start to get complicated when we have lines like `...foo.bar` or `....foo` and we may quickly lose count of the dots.

AFAICT, this replaces one call to `Get(CoarsenedTargets, ..)` with a call to `Get(TransitiveTargets, ..)` per partition.

If the partitions overlap at all (which I think will happen when inner nodes in the graph have very compatible ICs, while the roots are more constrainted), I expect that that will be slower due to #11270, because each `TransitiveTargets` request will do redundant re-computation of the dependencies.

An alternative would be to replace `closure: FrozenOrderedSet[Target]` with `root_targets: CoarsenedTargets`, and then call `root_targets.closure()` (which is still a transitive walk, but is entirely on the stack as opposed to spread across many `@rule` calls).

I think what we need is to think about what metadata & resources are related to each state. 

Dependency unready -> pending creation -> created -> restarting -> dead

Here, pending creation has technically two parts, scheduling and actor initialization, and actor initialization has more resources (processes started) to clean up. I assume we could have a similar issue when the state is restarting (but the mechanism we added here should handle this correctly). This PR adds code that implicitly distinguish actor initialization vs scheduling (by checking if the actor has an associated worker)

At the Reduce phase of GroupBy, in order to handle the `as_index` parameter properly, we need to know which columns were selected at the Map phase of the corresponding partition. Being in the Reduce phase we can't compute this anyhow (`df.columns` is not the answer, because some of the selected columns could have been dropped as non-suitable for this aggregation and so won't be presented in the `df.columns`).

The way to get this info at the Reduce phase is to precompute selection for every partition and explicitly pass these selections to each partition as a function parameter.

```
import time

          result = f(*args, **kw)
          te = time.time()

          logger.info(message % (te-ts))
          return result
    return timed
```

Everything but the item 2 was applied. But I'm not sure of what to do about objects in the module's namespace (not child objects) that fail to serialize. Consider these scenarios with two imported modules, `mod`  and `other_mod`, and an unpickleable variable named `var` that doesn't have an attribute `__name__` or `__qualname__` (note that there's no way to know where `var` was defined):

1.
    - `mod` defines `var`
    - `var` is not imported into other modules or is imported with aliases
    -  in `dump_module(module=mod)`, `var` may saved globally as `mod.var` or fail to save (e.g. for `__main__`)
2.
    - `mod` defines `var`
    - `other_mod` imports `var` from `mod`
    - in `dump_module(module=mod)`, `var` may saved globally as `mod.var` or `other_mod.var`
3.
    - `other_mod` defines `var`
    - `mod` imports `var` from `other_mod`
    - in `dump_module(module=mod)`, `var` may saved globally as `mod.var` or `other_mod.var`
4.
    - `other_mod` defines `var`
    - `mod` imports `var` as `var_alias`
    - in `dump_module(module=mod)`, `var` may saved globally as `mod.var_alias` or fail to save

**For each of these cases, which result is more appropriate?** Are all the desired behaviors compatible, i.e. is it possible to implement all of them consistently? Remember that, for the third scenario, `other_mod` may be a built-in module, which is safe, but may also be a third-party module not available in the unpickling environment. The behavior in this case can be stratified according to whether `other_mod` is a built-in module, is another module in the same package as `mod`, or is a third-party module.
